step.title: Select Shape (Recipe or Custom)
step.goal: Decide whether the ticket's structure follows the tag recipe's default shape, deviates with custom additions/omissions, or requires a fully custom outline. Also decide whether the ticket warrants numbered Parts (heavy ticket convention per spec §2.3).

substep.title: Load Tag Recipe
substep.action:
Look for a tag-recipe file at .vera/shared/references/tag-recipes/{{primary_tag}}.md. When Phase 4 is complete, these recipes specify per-tag section lists and content guidance.
If the recipe exists, parse: - Mandatory section list (in writing order) - Optional sections (per-ticket decision) - Notes on each section's purpose - Whether the recipe assumes numbered Parts
Store as {{tag_recipe}} with a 'present' or 'absent' flag.
If the recipe is absent, fall back to a built-in skeleton based on the primary tag. Defaults:
- feature: Problem Statement, User Stories, Behaviors, Edge Cases, Decision Records (linked), Acceptance Criteria, Out of Scope - bug: Reproduction Steps, Expected vs Actual, Root Cause Hypothesis (if known), Fix Approach, Acceptance Criteria, Out of Scope - exploration: Goal, Boundaries, Time Box, Expected Outputs, Decision Points, Out of Scope - hotfix: Incident Description, Fix, Rollback Plan, Verification, Postmortem Link - infrastructure: Current State, Target State, Setup Steps Required, Dependencies, Verification, Out of Scope - chore: What, Why, Acceptance Criteria, Out of Scope

substep.title: Decide on Numbered Parts
substep.action:
Heavy tickets (per spec §2.3) often use numbered Parts to group sections for section-batched review. Apply Parts when:
- The ticket is expected to exceed ~600 lines (estimated from the Design Summary's artifact-chain breadth and the primary tag) - The work spans >2 distinct concern areas (e.g., data model changes AND middleware changes AND API surface AND test migration) - The downstream implementation will clearly decompose into multiple slices (the Design Summary often surfaces this)
Standard Part patterns: - **Part 1: Context and Decisions** (problem, design summary, DR references) - **Part 2: Behavior and Contract** (user stories, behaviors, AC, scope boundaries) - **Part 3: Risk and Edge Cases** (risk areas, edge cases, rollout concerns) - **Part 4: Open Questions / Future Work** (only if applicable)
Store {{use_parts}}: true | false. If true, store {{part_plan}}: list of {part_number, part_title, sections_in_part}.

substep.title: Identify Custom Additions and Omissions
substep.action:
Compare the recipe's section list to what THIS ticket needs:
**Additions**: sections to add that the recipe doesn't mandate. Driven by: - Linked DRs (each DR's decision typically gets its own surfaced section) - Risk areas from the Design Summary (a major risk may warrant a dedicated section) - Prior thoughts/ artifacts that this ticket extends (cross-references section)
**Omissions**: recipe sections to skip. Driven by: - Sections that don't apply to this ticket's primary concern (e.g., a feature ticket that doesn't touch user-facing behavior may omit "User Stories" in favor of an "API Behaviors" section) - Recipe sections that duplicate content already in the Design Summary (avoid restating)
Each addition and omission must have a rationale. Store as {{custom_additions}} and {{custom_omissions}}, each as a list of {section_name, rationale}.
Anti-pattern guard: if additions exceed 50% of the recipe's sections, the recipe probably doesn't fit this ticket — switch to fully-custom mode (substep 2d).

substep.title: Decide Custom vs Recipe-Driven
substep.action:
Final shape decision: - **Recipe-driven**: recipe's sections work; <50% additions; no mode-flag needed - **Recipe-with-customizations**: recipe is the base; additions and omissions documented - **Fully-custom**: the recipe doesn't fit this ticket's nature; design a custom section list from scratch (justifying each section's inclusion)
Store as {{shape_mode}}.
If fully-custom, draft the custom section list now (will be refined in step 3): names + one-line scope notes per section. Aim for 5-12 sections; fewer is too thin, more is bloat.

substep.title: Compose Outline Draft
Display Shape Summary

section.title: When to use Parts
section.description: Numbered Parts exist to enable section-batched review. A 300-line ticket doesn't need them — read it whole. A 1200-line ticket NEEDS them — the user can't hold the full ticket in head, so review-by-Part is the practical strategy. Apply Parts when expected line count is over ~600 OR when concern areas cluster into 3+ groups.

rules.rule: Don't force Parts on small tickets — overhead with no benefit
Don't omit Parts on huge tickets — the user will lose the thread
Part titles describe what's IN the part, not the abstract role
4 Parts is the typical maximum; more suggests the ticket should decompose downstream
Section names + one-line scope notes are OK — they describe what the section is
Specifying section CONTENT is OUT OF SCOPE for this skill — that's ticket-write's job
Section ORDER is a structure decision, not a content decision — OK to lock
Section LENGTH expectations are guesses; surface in notes but don't lock specifics

section.title: Recipe-vs-custom tradeoff
section.description: Recipes encode pattern-matched best practices for typical tickets per tag. Custom outlines exist because real tickets sometimes don't fit the pattern. Bias toward the recipe when possible — consistency across tickets makes them easier to consume. Switch to custom when the recipe's assumptions are actively wrong for this ticket.
